frameworks.name: successor features and generalised policy improvement (SF&GPI)
frameworks.one_line: reuse decomposed task-value representations to evaluate prior policies for new tasks

frameworks.name: model-free perseveration
frameworks.one_line: stimulus-response habit reuse without task-value decomposition

frameworks.name: model-based control
frameworks.one_line: planning using a full environment model rather than cached policies

frameworks.name: successor representation
frameworks.one_line: predictive state representation underlying the SF&GPI decomposition

core_question: How do humans generalise solutions from past tasks to novel tasks, and is this implemented neurally?
key_assumptions: task value decomposes into reusable features and reward weights
candidate policies from prior tasks can be evaluated on new tasks
generalisation should be reward-selective rather than blanket reuse
manipulations: training tasks versus novel test tasks
reward structure across tasks supporting selective reuse
task configurations distinguishing SF&GPI from model-free and model-based predictions
key_findings: participants learned optimal training-task solutions and reused them on novel test tasks in a reward-selective way
choice behaviour matched SF&GPI predictions, not model-free perseveration or full model-based control
fMRI decoding showed SF&GPI solutions activated in visual and prefrontal cortex on test tasks
stronger visual-cortex SF&GPI activation tracked greater behavioural reuse
interpretations: humans generalise via an SF&GPI-like policy reuse algorithm rather than habit or full planning
visual and prefrontal cortex provide a candidate neural substrate for adaptive cross-task generalisation
